lines.text: The front steps of Ridgemont High. 9:52. Eight minutes to first bell.
Okay. Okay so. New school, new plan. I'm not 'new girl who cried in the car.'
I'm 'new girl who's mysterious and has places to be.'
Very chill. Very normal. Ten out of ten normal.
She catches her reflection in the trophy case just inside the doors — chin up, or chin trying to be.
Room 114. Room 114. You got this.
lines.action: straightens her jacket collar like armor

lines.text: The locker bay outside Room 114 is a wall of identical metal doors. Maya has now passed the same water fountain twice.
112... 112... okay, you are lying to me, locker 112, you do not exist—
A girl peels off from a knot of friends near the trophy case, grinning before she even opens her mouth.
Okay, spinning-in-circles girl — your locker's the one you're currently *not* standing in front of. Groundbreaking detective work.
A ripple of laughter moves down the hall. Somewhere in it, a quiet boy near the lockers flinches, like he's heard this exact song before.
Cool locker technique. Very avant-garde.
It's dry, and it's funny, and it's gone as fast as it came — Sam's already looking at his shoes.
Okay. Everyone's looking. Pick a face and wear it.
The cafeteria hits like a wall of noise and invisible property lines. Every table already belongs to somebody.
Find a table, don't cry, befriend at least one (1) person. Boom. Day one. Complete.
Maya, right? C'mon, sit — we don't bite. Mostly.
lines.action: waves an arm like claiming territory

lines.text: Across the room, near the recycling bins, a boy with headphones eats alone, one earbud in, not looking up. It's the same boy from the hallway.
By the window, an empty table waits, sunlight and all, for absolutely nobody.
Okay so. Ten minutes. Tray in hand. Pick a flag, plant it.
The table is loud in a way that feels like being plugged into something. Jordan holds court; everyone leans in like she's the only outlet in the room.
—so then Coach Reyes goes, 'this isn't a group chat, put the phone away,' like, my guy, respectfully, it's a calculator app—
The table laughs. Maya laughs too, a little relieved to have somewhere to put the laugh.
Okay, you're funny. I like her. New girl's funny, everybody clock that.
lines.action: arm sliding along the back of Maya's chair

lines.text: Across the cafeteria, Sam approaches with a full tray, headphones down around his neck, threading carefully between tables.
Oops.
lines.action: sticks out a foot, not even looking down

lines.text: The tray goes down loud — a full, ringing crash of plastic and spilled milk. The table erupts.
Oh my god — Sam, I'm sorry, I didn't even — okay that was not on purpose, that's so embarrassing for both of us—
Sam is frozen on the floor amid the wreckage, shoulders up around his ears, waiting for it to be over. Everyone's looking at Maya now, mid-laugh, to see what she'll do.
Maya's already out of her seat before she's decided to be.
Hey — you okay?
...Yeah. Tray's the real casualty here.
That wasn't cool, Jordan.
Jordan's smile tightens for half a second — then she shrugs it off, loud, like the moment never happened.
Relax, it was a bit. Wasn't it, Sam? ...Sam's fine. Look, he's already up.
Sam catches Maya's eye. He mouths one word — thanks — before gathering what's left of his lunch and heading for the trash cans.
Small thing. Didn't feel small.
The laugh is out of Maya's mouth before she decides to let it out — high, quick, swept up in the current of the table.
See? Told you she was funny.
lines.action: arm sliding along the back of Maya's chair

lines.text: Sam gathers his tray off the floor alone. He doesn't look back on his way to the trash cans.
That's fine, right? Everyone laughed. It's not like I—
Somehow him not looking back is worse than if he had.
Okay wait, is the pizza here always like this or did today just personally insult all of us?
It works — technically. The table's attention slides off the tray on the floor and onto cafeteria conspiracy theories.
Oh my god, don't get me started, the 'cheese' is a construct—
Sam picks himself up without a word. Nobody quite manages to look at him while he does it. Maya included.
That counts as handling it. ...Doesn't it?
Maya sets her tray down across from the boy with the headphones. He looks up like he's checking for a punchline.
...You're aware there are, statistically, better tables.
Yeah, well, statistically I don't know anyone yet, so. Low bar.
Fair.
Sam pulls his other earbud out the rest of the way, like he's deciding this is actually happening.
I'm Sam. I'd shake your hand but I don't want to jinx the tray staying upright twice in one day.
Smart. I'm Maya. New. Extremely new. Like, wet-paint new.
Don't worry, everyone does the spinny-confused-locker thing their first week. It's basically a school tradition. I did it for, like, a month.
A month? Did they move your locker on you?
No, I just have a bad sense of direction and a deep fear of asking for help. It's a whole personality.
Maya laughs — a real one this time. Sam looks briefly startled that it landed.
I used to sit with a bigger group. People got... busy. It's whatever.
Well. New friends starting today. Look at that, timing.
...Today, huh?
Today. I don't make the rules. Actually I do, I just made that one up.
Sam glances across the cafeteria — a table of kids from the art room, paint still on somebody's knuckles — and nods toward it, like he's already deciding something.
...They're not terrible. I've seen worse.
That one's his call. Not mine. I like that.
Hey. You don't have to fill that with anything, if you don't want to.
Sam glances at her, surprised — then relaxes, just slightly, shoulders coming down from around his ears.
...Okay.
They eat. It isn't awkward — it's easy, the specific ease of two people who've stopped performing for anyone. Halfway through, Sam slides a sketchbook across the table without a word.
Whoa. You draw?
I mess around.
This is really good, Sam.
...It's whatever.
Okay but — you don't have to do the whole rest of lunch solo. Go wave somebody over.
...Wave who over.
I don't know, you're the one with a whole flowchart of who's normal here.
Sam scans the room — then, before Maya can say anything else, he's half out of his seat.
Hey — you guys can sit with us, if you want.
Two kids from his old lunch table drift over, tentative. Sam looks almost as surprised as they do that it worked.
There it is. Not my move. His.
Maya sets her tray down at the empty table by the window, mostly to have somewhere to put it. Thirty seconds later, she's not alone anymore.
Hey, I'm Alex — they/them.
lines.action: already pulling out the chair across from her

lines.text: Oh — hi. Maya. Is this seat, like, taken, or—
Taken by future-us, statistically. Field report: table's empty, vibes are neutral, no known predators. I'm calling it. You in?
I mean, when you put it that way.
I've done the new-kid thing enough times to have a flowchart. Step one's always 'find the other person who also doesn't know where to sit.' Congrats. You're step one.
Wait, 'enough times' — how many schools are we talking?
Oh, four. Five if you count the one I was at for eleven days, which I don't, because eleven days isn't a school, it's a rumor.
Alex doodles a tiny, elaborate castle onto a napkin without seeming to notice they're doing it.

...Okay but real talk — I don't actually know if I'm still gonna be here in October. My mom's job is like that. So. No pressure, but if this table's gonna be a thing, can it be a thing starting today?
Anyway. I've been thinking about starting a thing — like a club, after school, doesn't matter what, just a room where showing up is the whole requirement. Might actually do it this year.
Room 114. Ms. Alvarez taps her clipboard twice with her pen, the way she does before she says anything that matters.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park — you three. Congratulations, you get each other for the semester. Try to enjoy it.
Across the room, Jordan catches Maya's eye for half a second, like she's trying to read which version of today just happened.
Sam doesn't look especially thrilled at the pairing. Given the last hour, it's hard to blame him.
Okay. Efficient. I've got some initial thoughts, we should divide labor by strength, I'll circulate an outline by tonight.
Ten minutes into knowing this kid and he's already circulating outlines. Noted.
Ms. Alvarez taps the clipboard twice, glancing at it a beat too long, like this pairing isn't as random as it looks.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park. You three, for the semester.
...Huh. Guess the universe agrees with lunch.
Devon's already got a notebook flipped open before his own name finishes being read.
Try to keep up.
Sam's grin doesn't move an inch.
Is he always like this?
We'll find out together.
Ms. Alvarez taps the clipboard twice.
Ferreira, Okafor, Park. Semester group.
Maya's stomach dips a little at 'Okafor' — a name she only knows as 'that quiet kid by the window.'
Devon is already flipping to a fresh page before his own name finishes being read.
Across the room, Alex catches Maya's eye and gives an exaggerated, sympathetic shrug.
Good luck.
Whatever table we built at lunch — this is a very different kind of group.
Library work table. Devon's already sketched a timeline on a legal pad like he's been waiting all period for this.
Okay, respectfully, here's the plan — poster board, split into three sections, timeline of causes, we hit every rubric point, we don't overthink it—
...Or — hear me out — what if instead of a poster, we did it like a fake news broadcast? Breaking-news style, we film it—
Respectfully, that's not going to work, we don't have editing software, we don't have time, and statistically the rubric rewards clarity over — no offense — production value.
Sam goes quiet in the specific way of someone who's used to being talked over. He looks down at the table, taps two fingers against the wood, once.
So — poster board. I can bring the supplies tomorrow, I've basically got the layout in my head already—
One sentence. That's what I've got.
Wait — I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.
...Respectfully — you haven't even heard the whole—
I heard enough. It's different, it's actually kind of memorable, and 'we've never done that before' isn't the same as 'it won't work.'
Devon opens his mouth, closes it. The idea's good enough that even he can't argue for long.
...Fine. Fine — if we storyboard it first, so we're not just filming randomly—
Yeah. Yeah, I can storyboard it tonight, actually—
It was never really about being defended. It was about being heard.
...Thanks.
Okay — what if we do both? Poster for the timeline, but Sam's broadcast thing for the actual presentation part.
...That could work. If the poster's still doing the heavy lifting on content—
—and the broadcast's just the hook, sure, that's fine with me.
The blended pitch takes both ideas and files off the sharpest edges. Devon looks vaguely satisfied at getting some credit; Sam looks quietly relieved his idea survived at all.
Fine. I'll draft the poster layout, Sam does the broadcast script — Maya, you're point on making sure they actually match.
...Wow, okay, promoted to referee. Living the dream.
Nobody's thrilled. Everybody can work with it. For a first day, that might be its own small win.
Devon's plan sails through unchallenged.
Great. Poster board, three sections, I'll bring supplies tomorrow.
Sam doesn't say another word for the rest of the period. He taps two fingers against the table, once, twice, then stops.
It's not a big deal. It's really not a big deal.
The knot in her stomach disagrees. Not because Sam needed saving — because a good idea just got buried, and nobody said so.
Bus loop. Late afternoon. Backpack on, day survived.
Okay. Not a disaster. Ten out of ten normal — okay, maybe a seven.
Jordan passes by on her way to a ride, and for half a second, drops the performance.
Hey — the tray thing, earlier. That was kind of messed up on my part.
lines.emotion: quieter than usual, not quite looking at her

lines.text: It's not quite an apology. It's not nothing, either.
...Yeah. It kind of was.
Anyway. See you tomorrow, new girl.
And then she's gone, back to being unbothered, like the last ten seconds didn't happen.
Maya's phone buzzes in her pocket.
Okay. Tomorrow starts here.
The bus loop empties out slow, one yellow bus at a time.
One text. That's all it takes to find out if today was real or just a nice thing that happened once.
The reply comes back fast — faster than Maya expected.
Maya's phone lights up — a group chat already mid-conversation, her name added somewhere in the middle of it.
Huh. That was fast.
It feels good. Loud, easy, instantly visible.
Maya stares at the blank text box longer than she means to.
Bigger risk than picking a side. Also maybe the only option where nobody eats alone.

options.text: Fire back with a confident joke of your own
"Rough first day for everyone, huh?" (nudge the attention off Sam)
Laugh along and play the good sport

a.text: Cute. I'll have you know I've been rehearsing that spin for style points, not accuracy.
Oh, she's got jokes. Okay. I like her.
The hallway laughs — with her this time, not at her.

b.text: Rough first day for everyone, huh?
b.emotion: easy, glancing at Sam then back

b.text: Sam's shoulders drop half an inch. Jordan blinks, thrown for half a second by the redirect.
...Sure. Whatever. Locker's that way, by the way.

c.text: Maya laughs along, a beat too loud, the way you laugh when you're deciding it doesn't sting.
See, she gets it. Locker's that way.
That was fine. That was totally fine.

options.text: Head for Jordan's table
Sit across from Sam
Take the empty table by the window

a.text: Maya angles her tray toward the noise, toward the center of the room.

b.text: Maya crosses the cafeteria toward the boy with the headphones, tray held like a decision she's already made.
Okay. Quiet table. Worth a shot.

c.text: Maya sets her tray down at the empty table by the window, alone, for now.
Okay. My own table. See who shows up.

options.text: Help Sam up and tell Jordan that wasn't cool
Laugh along with the table
Change the subject smoothly

b.text: Maya laughs before she can stop herself.

c.text: Okay wait, is the pizza here always like this—

options.text: "New friends. Starting today."
Just let the quiet be enough
"Go wave somebody over. Right now."

a.text: Well. New friends starting today. Look at that, timing.

b.text: Hey. You don't have to fill that with anything.

c.text: Okay but — go wave somebody over. Right now.

options.text: Get excited — start inviting people over now
Keep it low-key, just the two of you
Admit you're still curious about Jordan's table

a.text: Okay wait, we should just... start now. Table's got room.
Oh, see, THIS is the energy I was hoping for—

b.text: Honestly? I kind of just want today to be this. Just this.
...Yeah. Okay. I can do just this.

c.text: ...I don't know, part of me's still wondering what that table's like.
The loud one? Oh, I've got notes on the loud one. Several notes.

options.text: "Wait, I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying."
Propose a compromise blending both ideas
Stay quiet and let Devon's plan move forward

a.text: Wait — I actually think Sam's idea is worth trying.

b.text: Okay — what if we do both?

c.text: Maya opens her mouth, then closes it, and lets the moment slide past.

options.text: Text Sam: sit together again tomorrow?
Accept Jordan's invite to the crowd tomorrow
Text Sam a bigger idea — everyone at one table

c.text: ok hear me out — bigger table tomorrow?

lines.text: hey. sit with me again tomorrow?
Wait you actually texted me!! I mean — cool. Cool cool cool. What's up.
lines.emotion: one exclamation point, clearly not his usual style

lines.text: It's not the whole school. It's one person who's glad I showed up.
bring the sketchbook. I want to see how the comic ends.
...deal.
Not a big win. A real one.
table's not the same without you, sit with us tmrw obviously
Easy. That was easy. So why does the tray hitting the floor keep replaying?
I laughed. Sam didn't look back. That's the part that's still sitting there.
yeah, see you tomorrow
lines.action: typing, then deleting, then typing again

lines.text: you're funny AND you've got a spine, respect. table tomorrow?
I told her off for the tray thing. And I'm still saying yes to her table.
Maybe both things can be true today. Maybe I just haven't figured out which one wins yet.
...yeah. tomorrow.
table tomorrow, obviously
Nothing blew up today. Nothing really got fixed, either.
Sam's tray. Sam's idea. Both just... quietly went away. And I let them.
sounds good
lines.emotion: typing without hesitation, which is somehow the unsettling part

lines.text: ok hear me out. tomorrow — what if our table has room for more people. you, me, alex, maybe jordan if he can chill for like five minutes
...jordan? bold choice.
he apologized. sort of. baby steps.
fine. but if he trips ME I'm suing
Not a side. A table. One that's actually big enough.

voiceNotes: Jordan speaks for an audience even one-on-one — quick, tagged with names, laughing right after the cruel part to retroactively make it a joke ('Oops.' / 'that's so embarrassing for BOTH of us'). Her one sincere line at the bus loop is deliberately unpolished and short, because sincerity is unfamiliar territory for her — she can't sustain it past two lines before reflexively snapping back to performance mode. Sam undersells everything ('I mess around,' 'It's whatever') — his real feeling shows in the taps, the stillness, the one unguarded 'thanks,' never in a direct statement of hurt. When something matters to him (the broadcast idea), his sentences get faster and more concrete — a rare break from his usual short, armored replies. Devon never says 'I was wrong,' he says 'that could work' or goes quiet — his tell is the missing interruption, not an apology. Alex self-interrupts and narrates their own awkwardness as a defense, but the 'real talk' line is flagged in-text because it's the one moment they drop the bit entirely, which should feel like a small tonal seatbelt-click for the reader. Maya's internal voice is faster and more sarcastic than her spoken lines — the gap between what she thinks and what she says is the whole game.

alternatives: New locker performance art. I give it a nine.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Sam's dry, formal-word deflection style if 'avant-garde' reads too on-the-nose for some players; both alternatives preserve the joke-then-vanish rhythm.

alternatives: Hey. Earlier — the tray thing. Not my finest.
So the tray thing was kind of a jerk move. Yeah.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Jordan's apology needs to stay clipped and slightly deflected rather than fully owned — these alternatives keep the performative distance intact if the primary line reads too earnest.

alternatives: Anyway. Semi-serious club idea, don't laugh — a room after school where the only rule is you showed up. Might do it this year, might not. Depends on the mood of the universe.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Longer, more tangential version if Alex's rapid self-interruption pattern needs more emphasis in performance.
